text.title: A/B Testing Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common A/B testing failures: random assignment, missing exposures, peeking, wrong statistical test.

antipattern.reason: Produces a different variant on every page load, request, or process restart. The same user alternates between variants, cross-contaminating both groups and making results meaningless.

antipattern.description: Running successive experiments on the same population without changing the hash salt (experiment ID).
antipattern.reason: Correlated assignment: users in control for experiment 1 are always in control for experiment 2. This produces correlated bias that looks like real effects.

antipattern.description: Stopping the experiment the moment the p-value crosses 0.05 (peeking).
antipattern.reason: Multiple comparisons over time inflate the false-positive rate. Stopping on first significance yields a 20–30% false-positive rate instead of the intended 5%.

antipattern.description: Using `scipy.stats.ttest_ind` on a binary (0/1) conversion metric.
antipattern.reason: The t-test assumes approximately normal data. Binary metrics violate this for small or imbalanced samples, producing incorrect p-values. Use chi-square or two-proportion z-test.

antipattern.description: Logging conversions without logging exposures, then computing conversion rate as conversions / total_users.
antipattern.reason: Users not exposed to the experiment are included in the denominator, diluting the conversion rate and obscuring real effects. Denominator must be unique exposed users per variant.
